meta.title: What's Your Social Media Style?
meta.subtitle: You run your own business's social media. This reads how you actually show up—and where it's quietly costing you growth.
meta.note: There are no wrong answers. Every option is a real, legitimate way to run your own social. Answer honestly—the result reads your pattern, not your best guess at the right choice.

questions.prompt: When you sit down to post for your business, where does the idea usually come from?

options.label: The story I'm telling about my business right now—the next piece of it.
A question my customers keep asking me.
Something my followers are talking about or going through.
A trend or format that's taking off this week.
Whatever I need to sell or book this month.

questions.prompt: Your posting plan, honestly, looks like…

options.label: A loose storyline—I know the chapters I want to share over time.
A list of topics I know my audience needs help with.
Whatever feels right in the moment for my community.
Mostly open—I fill it with whatever's trending when I sit down.
Built around my promotions, launches, and offers.

questions.prompt: What actually gets you to post consistently?

options.label: I don't want to drop the story I've already started telling.
I batch and reuse templates, so posting is a system, not a scramble.
The people who expect to hear from me keep me showing up.
Staying active is how I keep the algorithm on my side.
There's always something to sell, so there's always a reason to post.

questions.prompt: It's a crazy week. What's the first thing to slip?

options.label: The extras—but I keep the posts that move my story forward.
Nothing new—I just re-share an older tip that still helps.
I drop to quick stories and replies, just to stay present.
I throw up a fast trend post to keep the streak alive.
I protect whatever post is tied to money this week.

questions.prompt: What makes your account recognizably yours?

options.label: My voice and my story—you'd know it's me with the name covered.
I actually know my stuff, and it shows in what I share.
It feels like a real person, not a faceless business account.
I put my own spin on trends and make them look good.
It's clear—people always know what I offer and how to get it.

questions.prompt: Another business in your niche copies your style. What can't they take?

options.label: My story—it's mine to tell.
The real experience behind what I share.
The relationship I've built with my followers.
My speed—I'll already be onto the next trend.
My offer and my results—that's what sells, not the look.

questions.prompt: How do new people usually find your business on social?

options.label: Someone shares my story because it hit home for them.
A helpful post gets saved and sent to a friend.
Word of mouth from followers who feel like regulars.
A reel or trend post took off and reached new people.
I put content in front of people who are ready to buy.

questions.prompt: Which feels most like your platform game plan?

options.label: Go deep on the one or two platforms where my story lands.
Wherever my audience is learning—I adapt the tip to fit.
Wherever the conversation is, so I can jump in.
Wherever I can get the most reach right now.
Wherever my actual buyers spend their time.

questions.prompt: How do you know your posting is actually working?

options.label: People message me saying my story stuck with them.
Saves, shares, and 'this helped me so much' replies.
More DMs, more comments, more regulars showing up.
My reach and follower count are climbing.
I can point to leads, bookings, or sales it brought in.

questions.prompt: A friend asks, "is social media even worth it for your business?" What do you point to?

options.label: People remember my business when it counts.
I'm becoming the name people trust in my niche.
I've built a small community that has my back.
How many new people I'm reaching every month.
The customers and revenue I can trace back to it.

questions.prompt: One post blows up—10x your usual likes. But nothing else changes: no new messages, no saves, no sales. How do you take it?

options.label: A nice spike—but only useful if I can turn those new eyes into something real.
Good for getting seen. I note it and keep my real goals in mind.
A win is a win—the like count is the number that matters most.
Probably just the algorithm. I won't change my whole approach over one post.
